alignments.pub_claim_text: The predicted closed and open states closely resemble crystal structures determined under resting and activating conditions, and project to predicted basins in free energy landscapes calculated from Markov state models. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the core resemblance claim but clarifies/extends it by adding that the states project to basins in MSM-derived free energy landscapes.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method provides an efficient way to predict stable conformations and accurately sample transition pathways several orders of magnitude faster than simulation-based sampling. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This approach provides an efficient way to predict stable conformations and sample transition pathways several orders of magnitude faster than simulation-based sampling. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same efficiency claim about predicting conformations and sampling transition pathways with similar framing and confidence.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The AlphaFold2 sampling captures intermediate conformations and accurately projects onto the transition pathway resolved in extensive sampling. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds an explicit claim about capturing intermediate conformations and projecting onto transition pathways that was not stated in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A deep learning-based drug repositioning strategy was employed to screen potential anti-SARS-CoV-2 drug candidates from 2,635 FDA-approved drugs and 1,062 active ingredients from Traditional Chinese Medicine herbs. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A deep learning-based drug repositioning strategy was employed to screen potential anti-SARS-CoV-2 drug candidates from 2635 FDA-approved drugs and 1062 active ingredients from Traditional Chinese Medicine herbs. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same screening strategy and dataset; numbers and scope match.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: In silico molecular docking analysis validates the interactions between the top compounds and host receptors or viral spike proteins. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: In silico molecular docking analysis validates the interactions between the top compounds and host receptors or viral spike proteins. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical claim and confidence between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Several drug candidates including Fostamatinib, Linagliptin, Lysergol, and Sophoridine can effectively block the cell entry of SARS-CoV-2 variants into human lung cells even at a nanomolar scale. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Several drug candidates including Fostamatinib, Linagliptin, Lysergol, and Sophoridine can effectively block the cell entry of SARS-CoV-2 variants into human lung cells even at a nanomolar scale. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result is restated verbatim with the same level of certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study illuminates the feasibility of applying deep learning-based drug repositioning for antiviral agents by targeting a specified mechanism. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study illuminates the feasibility of applying deep learning-based drug repositioning for antiviral agents by targeting a specified mechanism. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The concluding claim is preserved unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ExoGRU is a machine learning model that predicts small RNA secretion probabilities from primary RNA sequence. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ExoGRU is a machine learning model that predicts small RNA secretion probabilities from primary RNA sequences. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same core contribution with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Primary RNA sequence is a major determinant in small RNA secretion. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The performance of ExoGRU was experimentally validated through ExoGRU-guided mutagenesis and synthetic RNA sequence analysis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides experimental validation of model performance (new supporting evidence), which clarifies and strengthens the preprint's claim about the importance of sequence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ExoGRU revealed cis and trans factors that underlie small RNA secretion, including known and novel RNA-binding proteins. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ExoGRU revealed cis and trans factors that underlie small RNA secretion, including known and novel RNA-binding proteins. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same finding but reports it with higher confidence (experimental validation and stronger wording).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The novel technique exoCLIP reveals the RNA interactome of RNA-binding proteins within the cell-free space. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The novel technique exoCLIP reveals the RNA interactome of RNA-binding proteins within the cell-free space. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The contribution is reproduced unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The insights gained from this study can be leveraged in therapeutic and synthetic biology applications. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The knowledge gained from this research can be leveraged in therapeutic and synthetic biology applications. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The concluding applicability statement is preserved with equivalent scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning models are effective for biomarker discoveries in clinical metabolomics studies. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning models are effective for biomarker discoveries in clinical metabolomics studies. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same high-level result verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Tree-based Shapley Additive explanations (SHAP) provide more explanation depth than PLS-DA’s Variable Influence in Projection (VIP) scores. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SHAP offers greater explanation depth compared to the Variable Influence in Projection (VIP) scores used in PLS-DA. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same comparative claim about SHAP vs VIP with equivalent emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The use of SHAP enhances the interpretability of machine learning models in metabolomics studies. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The use of SHAP enhances the interpretability of machine learning predictions in metabolomics studies. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication shifts wording from 'models' to 'predictions' (a slight clarification) but preserves the intended conclusion and confidence.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Tree-based Shapley Additive explanations (SHAP) provide local explanation properties for machine learning models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication introduces an explicit characterization of SHAP as providing local explanation properties, which was not stated in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Random Forest algorithm outperformed the other two algorithms in identifying correct 3D docking models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Random Forest algorithm outperformed the other two algorithms (Supported Vector Machine and Perceptron) in identifying correct 3D docking models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result but adds the specific comparator algorithms, which clarifies the claim without changing its scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CoDES, the trained random forest classifier, demonstrates state-of-the-art ability to discriminate correct from incorrect docking decoys. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CoDES demonstrates state-of-the-art ability to discriminate correct from incorrect docking decoys based on global parameters and ranking. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the 'state-of-the-art' discrimination claim and adds detail about the basis (global parameters and ranking), so the claim is clarified without changing strength.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The performance of CoDES was validated on independent datasets and compared favorably with other machine-learning methods in the field. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The COnservation Driven Expert System (CoDES) was trained and validated using a features selection algorithm and optimized random forest hyperparameters. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes training/validation procedure and optimization rather than explicitly stating validation on independent datasets and favorable comparisons, so the original validation/comparison claim is revised and narrowed in scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The classifier achieved 70% generalization accuracy in distinguishing major depressive disorder (MDD) patients from healthy controls. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: No publication claim reports a 70% generalization accuracy for MDD vs controls; the specific classification accuracy claim is absent in the published paper.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The prediction model moderately well predicted symptom severity for an independent validation dataset. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The brain network prediction model significantly predicted Beck Depression Inventory-II (BDI) score for an independent validation dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms prediction on an independent dataset but frames it as a significant prediction of BDI score (stronger wording), so the claim is clarified and its confidence appears amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study successfully generalized findings to an independent dataset acquired from multiple imaging sites, ensuring scientific reproducibility and clinical applicability. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not make the strong multi-site generalization/reproducibility claim; instead it emphasizes limitations in relating diagnostics to neurobiology, so the original generalization claim is not retained.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Common functional connections were found between those related to MDD diagnosis and those related to depression symptoms. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Three common functional connections were found between those related to depression symptoms and those related to major depressive disorder (MDD) diagnosis. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result (three shared functional connections) with essentially identical meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning-based models developed using sequence composition achieved a maximum MCC of 0.63. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine learning-based models developed in this study achieved a maximum MCC of 0.63. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The published claim matches the preprint result verbatim, preserving meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Models developed using evolutionary information in the form of PSSM composition achieved a maximum MCC value of 0.66. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Models using evolutionary information in the form of PSSM composition achieved a maximum MCC value of 0.66. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same performance result with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The hybrid model that combined a similarity-based approach using BLAST and machine learning-based models achieved a maximum MCC value of 0.82 with an AUROC value of 0.95. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The hybrid model that combined BLAST and PSSM based model achieved a maximum MCC value of 0.82 with an AUROC value of 0.95. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same hybrid-model performance metrics; wording differences are minor and do not change the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A web server 'PRRpred' was developed based on the best model from this study to facilitate the scientific community. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A web server 'PRRpred' was developed based on the best model to facilitate the scientific community. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the contribution of a web server based on the best model with equivalent scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed method generates more accurate tertiary structure models than those produced from alignments obtained by other methods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed method generates more accurate tertiary structure models than those produced from alignments obtained by other methods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces the same high-confidence result about tertiary structure accuracy without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method trains a machine learning model using the structural alignment of known homologs. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The method trains a machine learning model using the structural alignment of known homologs to improve sequence alignments. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds the explicit goal (to improve sequence alignments), clarifying the contribution; the core claim about training on structural alignments is preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This method dynamically predicts a substitution score from the trained model when calculating sequence alignments. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This method dynamically predicts a substitution score from the trained model instead of using a fixed substitution matrix. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication clarifies the mechanism by contrasting it with using a fixed substitution matrix; the essence of dynamic prediction remains the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A machine learning model has been developed that can accurately predict the optimal growth temperature (OGT) for bacteria, archaea, and microbial eukaryotes based on their proteome-wide 2-mer amino acid composition. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A machine learning model has been developed that can accurately predict the optimal growth temperature (OGT) for bacteria, archaea, and microbial eukaryotes based on their proteome-wide 2-mer amino acid composition. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same high-confidence result about OGT prediction without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The second machine learning model developed using OGT data and amino acid composition provides enzyme catalytic temperature optima (T opt) estimates that are far superior to those obtained using OGT alone. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The resulting model for predicting T<sub>opt</sub> generates estimates that are far superior to those obtained using OGT alone. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the claim that the Topt prediction model substantially outperforms using OGT alone; wording and placement differ but meaning is the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study predicts T opt for 6.5 million enzymes across 4,447 enzyme classes and makes this dataset available for researchers. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: T<sub>opt</sub> has been predicted for 6.5 million enzymes across 4447 enzyme classes, and the resulting data set is available to researchers. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication gives the same quantitative contribution (predictions for 6.5M enzymes across 4447 classes) and availability, matching the preprint claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This work enables the simple and rapid identification of enzymes that are potentially functional at extreme temperatures. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly state the practical enabling claim about rapid identification of extremophile enzymes; it focuses on model availability and performance, so this specific applied conclusion is not retained.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning predictions averaged AUROCs of 0.97, 0.96, and 0.95 for 12-, 24-, and 48-hour predictions, respectively, when trained and tested on data from a single institution.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Gradient-boosted trees (GBT) achieved average AUROCs of 0.96, 0.95, and 0.94 for 12-, 24-, and 48-hour mortality predictions when trained and tested on data from the same institution.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports very similar AUROCs but slightly different values and specifies the algorithm (GBT), so the claim content was revised/updated rather than unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The algorithm achieved AUROC up to 0.95, 0.93, and 0.91 for 12-, 24-, and 48-hour predictions, respectively, when trained and tested on data from different hospitals.
alignments.pub_claim_text: GBT AUROCs reached up to 0.98, 0.96, and 0.96 for 12-, 24-, and 48-hour predictions, respectively, when trained and tested on data from different hospitals.
alignments.rationale: Both claims concern cross-hospital AUROCs but the publication gives different (higher) maxima and specifies GBT, indicating a revision of the reported values and method.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: MEWS and qSOFA had average 48-hour AUROCs of 0.86 and 0.82, respectively.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The average AUROC for 48-hour predictions for logistic regression (LR), support vector machine (SVM), Modified Early Warning Score (MEWS), and quick Sepsis-Related Organ Failure Assessment (qSOFA) were 0.85, 0.79, 0.86, and 0.82, respectively.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same MEWS and qSOFA values but adds LR and SVM results, clarifying and broadening the set of compared models.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This algorithm may help identify patients in need of increased levels of clinical care.
alignments.pub_claim_text: GBT predictions may help identify patients who would benefit from increased clinical care.
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is maintained but the publication specifies the model (GBT), so the claim is clarified rather than changed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: No single learning algorithm performs optimally for all data sets.
alignments.pub_claim_text: No single learning algorithm performs optimally for all data sets in predicting retention time.
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the same finding but makes it specific to the domain of retention time prediction, narrowing the scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Different algorithm types achieve top performance for different types of analytes or different protocols.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Different types of algorithms achieve top performance for different types of analytes or different protocols.
alignments.rationale: The statement is essentially the same, but the publication expresses it with lower certainty (medium), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Blending different types of models together decreases the error on outliers.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Blending different types of models together decreases the error on outliers.
alignments.rationale: The claim is identical but is presented with higher certainty/result status in the publication (amplified confidence).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The combination of several approaches holds substantial promise for the development of more generic, high-performing algorithms.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The combination of several approaches holds substantial promise for the development of more generic, high-performing algorithms.
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is carried forward unchanged in wording and confidence.

alignments.pub_claim_text: An evaluation of machine learning algorithms for retention time prediction is needed to find a suitable algorithm for specific analytes or protocols.
alignments.rationale: This recommendation/need appears in the publication but was not stated in the preprint, so it is an added claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A flexible model can attain a lower generalization error than an inflexible model in appropriate settings.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A flexible model can attain a lower generalization error than an inflexible model in appropriate settings.
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Trauma Severity Model (TSM) demonstrates superior performance compared to three established risk prediction models.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Trauma Severity Model (TSM) demonstrates superior predictive performance compared to three established risk prediction models.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same result, phrased as superior predictive performance; content is consistent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed TSM provides improved risk prediction for trauma patients.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Trauma Severity Model (TSM) demonstrates superior predictive performance compared to three established risk prediction models.
alignments.rationale: This conclusion is represented by the same publication result showing TSM's superior predictive performance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model improves over established models for each performance metric considered.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Trauma Severity Model (TSM) demonstrates superior predictive performance compared to three established risk prediction models.
alignments.rationale: The preprint claims improvement across each metric, but the publication report states overall superior predictive performance without explicitly claiming improvement on every single metric, so the claim is tempered and narrowed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SOFisher consistently outperformed the conventional approach across various metrics.
alignments.pub_claim_text: SOFisher consistently outperformed the conventional approach across various metrics.
alignments.rationale: The result is carried into the publication without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SOFisher's robustness and generalizability were validated through cross-domain generalization tests.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The guided experiment design of spatial single-omics on a small number of FOVs yielded insights into AD-related cell states, subtypes, and gene programs previously obtained through spatial multi-omics experiments on large tissue slices.
alignments.rationale: The publication demonstrates generalizability in a specific experimental context (insights from few FOVs comparable to large slices), which is a narrower, concrete instantiation of the preprint's broader generalizability claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SOFisher successfully guided the selection of FOVs containing neurofibrillary tangles and amyloid-β plaques in both single and dual target tissue landmark scenarios.
alignments.pub_claim_text: SOFisher successfully guided the selection of FOVs containing neurofibrillary tangles and amyloid-β plaques in both single and dual target tissue landmark scenarios.
alignments.rationale: This specific result is preserved verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SOFisher-guided experiment design of spatial single-omics on limited tissue areas yielded insights into AD-related cell states, subtypes, and gene programs.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The guided experiment design of spatial single-omics on a small number of FOVs yielded insights into AD-related cell states, subtypes, and gene programs previously obtained through spatial multi-omics experiments on large tissue slices.
alignments.rationale: The publication makes the claim more specific and compares the insights to prior large-slice experiments; it also reports the result with higher certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SOFisher has the potential to revolutionize the experiment design of spatial biology.
alignments.pub_claim_text: SOFisher has the potential to revolutionize the experiment design of spatial biology.
alignments.rationale: The forward-looking conclusion is carried through without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A subset of ventral pallidum (VP) neurons encoded reward prediction errors (RPEs) more robustly than those in the nucleus accumbens.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A subset of ventral pallidum (VP) neurons encoded reward prediction errors (RPEs) more robustly than the nucleus accumbens.
alignments.rationale: The core result is preserved with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: VP RPEs predicted trial-by-trial task engagement in rats.
alignments.pub_claim_text: VP RPEs predicted changes in task engagement.
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses the same predictive relationship slightly more generally but the substance is the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Optogenetic inhibition of VP reduced subsequent task-related reward seeking.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Optogenetic manipulation of the VP during reward delivery bidirectionally altered rats’ subsequent reward-seeking behavior.
alignments.rationale: The publication expands the manipulation result from unidirectional inhibition to bidirectional effects (manipulation alters behavior in both directions), broadening the reported effect.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The activity of VP RPE cells adapted when rewards were delivered in blocks, consistent with reinforcement learning.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The data suggest a pivotal role for the VP in computing teaching signals that influence adaptive reward seeking.
alignments.rationale: The publication offers a broader, higher-level interpretation (VP computes teaching signals) based on the data; this generalizes the preprint's specific adaptation observation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: History- and cue-based RPEs were largely separate across the VP neural population.
alignments.rationale: This specific claim about separation of RPE types is not represented in the publication's listed claims and appears to have been omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed model requires both choice and cost mechanisms to generate bout-and-pause patterns. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Both the choice and cost mechanisms are required to generate bout-and-pause patterns. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication claim restates the same result with equivalent scope and certainty; wording is effectively identical.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: If either the choice or cost mechanism is removed, the model fails to generate bout-and-pause patterns. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: If either the choice or cost mechanism is knocked out, the model does not generate bout-and-pause patterns. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same counterfactual/result language and certainty; wording change is minor.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model reproduces the relationships between experimental manipulations and the three components of bout-and-pause patterns reported in previous studies. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed model reproduces the relationships between experimental manipulations and the three components of bout-and-pause patterns reported by previous studies. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result claim about reproducing previously reported relationships with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Alternative models can also generate bout-and-pause patterns if they implement the choice and cost mechanisms. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Alternative models can generate bout-and-pause patterns as long as they implement the two mechanisms. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication makes the same conditional conclusion about alternative models requiring the two mechanisms; meaning and certainty are preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Participants were more tolerant of non-rewarded outcomes when these resulted from execution errors versus when execution was successful but the reward was withheld. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Participants were more tolerant of non-rewarded outcomes when these resulted from execution errors versus when execution was successful, but reward was withheld. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the behavioral result with equivalent phrasing and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A model-driven analysis of neural activity revealed an attenuation of the signal associated with negative reward prediction error in the striatum following execution failures. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A model-driven analysis of neural activity revealed an attenuation of the signal associated with negative reward prediction errors in the striatum following execution failures. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The neural finding is reported identically in the publication with the same scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Prediction errors in the mesostriatal dopamine system integrate high-level information during the evaluation of instantaneous reward outcomes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Prediction errors in the mesostriatal dopamine system integrate high-level information during the evaluation of instantaneous reward outcomes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conceptual conclusion about prediction errors is unchanged between versions in wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our model can distinguish non-recurred from recurred patients with an f1 score of 0.83. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our model can distinguish non-recurred from recurred patients with an f1 score of 0.88. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The core claim is the same but the reported F1 score changed (0.83 -> 0.88), which is a substantive numerical revision that strengthens the reported performance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Features that are the most predictive of recurrence include large and irregularly shaped tumor cell nuclei. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Features that are the most predictive of recurrence include large and irregularly shaped tumor cell nuclei. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The feature importance result is restated identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our work underlines the value of histological images in predicting treatment outcomes for DLBCL. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our work underlines the value of histological images in predicting treatment outcomes for DLBCL. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The concluding statement about the utility of histological images is unchanged in content and certainty.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Understanding the model’s decision could eventually result in novel treatments for DLBCL. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This forward-looking translational claim does not appear in the preprint and is a novel, lower-certainty conclusion added in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The CHiMP tools effectively classify images related to experimental outcomes in protein crystallisation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The classification network assigns images into categories relating to experimental outcomes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both claims describe the same classification capability and outcome categories; wording is equivalent in scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The object detection and instance segmentation networks provide accurate masks for individual crystals and crystallisation droplets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The tools perform object detection and instance segmentation, allowing the positions and sizes of crystals and crystallization droplets to be recorded. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes the functional outcome (positions/sizes recorded) rather than explicitly repeating 'accurate masks,' which clarifies how the detection/segmentation is used.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The integration of two CHiMP tools at the VMXi beamline eliminates the need for user input in monitoring crystallisation experiments. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Two of the tools are integrated at the VMXi macromolecular crystallography beamline, potentially eliminating the need for user input. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the integration but softens the absolute claim ('eliminates' -> 'potentially eliminating') and reduces certainty, so the claim is clarified and tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The third CHiMP tool is being integrated into the XChem platform to automate the targeting of compound dispensing into crystallisation droplets. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The third tool is being integrated into the XChem platform to enable automatic targeting of acoustic compound dispensing. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both describe the same ongoing integration to enable automatic targeting of compound dispensing; differences are minor wording.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The CHiMP tools were created for the analysis of micrographs of protein crystallization experiments. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication includes an explicit statement of the tools' intended purpose/creation that was not listed among the preprint claims; this is an added contribution-level claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We propose a novel approach that combines molecular evolution with machine learning to optimize protein engineering. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We propose a novel approach that combines molecular evolution with machine learning to optimize protein engineering. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same methodological contribution claim with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our approach enables the preparation of a small library suited for screening experiments with high enrichment of functional proteins. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our approach enables the preparation of a small library suited for screening experiments with high enrichment of functional proteins. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result about enabling a small, enriched screening library is restated identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We successfully obtained a number of proteins showing yellow fluorescence, 12 of which had better fluorescence performance than the reference yellow fluorescent protein (YFP). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We successfully obtained a number of proteins showing yellow fluorescence, 12 of which had longer wavelengths than the reference yellow fluorescent protein (YFP). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same set of proteins but changes the specific attribute of the 12 variants (from 'better fluorescence performance' to 'longer wavelengths'), which is a substantive revision of the measured property.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proof-of-concept demonstrated that our approach can alter the reference green fluorescent protein (GFP) to change its fluorescence to yellow while improving its fluorescence intensity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We demonstrated a proof-of-concept by altering the reference green fluorescent protein (GFP) to change its fluorescence to yellow. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the proof-of-concept (changing GFP to yellow) but omits the claim of improved fluorescence intensity, narrowing the claim and reducing its strength.

alignments.pub_claim_text: These results show the potential of our approach as a powerful method for directed evolution of fluorescent proteins. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This broader concluding statement about the method's potential is not explicitly listed among the preprint claims and appears as an added interpretive conclusion in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study analyzes about 35000 primary structures of natural proteins longer than 360 residues and a similarly sized set of randomized sequences. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study analyzes approximately 35,000 primary structures of natural proteins longer than 360 residues and a similarly sized set of randomized sequences. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Wording and content are effectively identical between preprint and publication; no substantive change detected.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A time series is formed from hydropathy values of the first 360 residues for each sequence composed of 20 amino acids. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A time series is formed from hydropathy values of the first 360 residues for each sequence composed of 20 amino acids. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The claim is effectively unchanged in content; only the label (result vs contribution) differs but the substance matches exactly.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A 1-hidden layer neural network configuration is developed to classify natural and random sequences. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A two-hidden layer neural network configuration is developed to classify natural and random protein sequences. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The overall contribution (a neural network classifier) is the same, but the architecture detail changes from one to two hidden layers, representing a revision of the methodological claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The neural network achieves a distinguishing accuracy of 88% between random and natural sequences. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The neural network achieves a distinguishing accuracy of 88% between natural and random sequences. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The reported performance number and conclusion are the same in preprint and publication with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Sequencing and data-processing methods had a substantial impact on stressor prediction. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Feature selection significantly improved performance for omics-based methods but not for amplicon sequencing. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The broad preprint claim that sequencing and data-processing methods impact prediction is narrowed in the paper to a specific data-processing step (feature selection) and its differential effect, clarifying which methods were influential.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: 16S sequencing outperformed all other sequencing methods in terms of stressor prediction based on the Matthews Correlation Coefficient. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Amplicon sequencing outperformed all other sequencing methods in terms of stressor prediction based on the Matthews Correlation Coefficient. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result that amplicon/16S sequencing outperformed other methods; the content is consistent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Omics-based methods performed poorly overall, likely due to insufficient sequencing depth. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not assert that omics methods performed poorly due to depth; instead it reports differences in diversity detection and improvements with feature selection, so this specific explanation is not present.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Amplicon sequencing might be a better candidate for machine-learning-based environmental stressor prediction than omics-based methods. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Amplicon sequencing might be a better candidate for machine-learning-based environmental stressor prediction than omics-based methods. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about amplicon sequencing being a better candidate is present verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: More sampling could improve stressor prediction performance. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not make an explicit claim about increased sampling improving performance, so this suggestion from the preprint is not carried through.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Omics-based methods detected a higher diversity of taxa than amplicon sequencing. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This result about taxonomic diversity detected by omics methods is present in the publication but was not stated in the preprint.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Even the highest observed performance for 16S sequencing was still only moderate. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds nuance that 16S performance, while best among methods, remained only moderate; this nuance was not present in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Mouse Grimace Scale (MGS) is an accurate and reliable tool for assessing pain in mice. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Mouse Grimace Scale (MGS) is a valuable tool for assessing pain in mice. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the MGS as a 'valuable tool' rather than asserting 'accurate and reliable,' softening the strength of the claim while preserving its utility.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A semi-automated pipeline has been developed as a first step towards fully automated surveillance of well-being in mice. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed semi-automated pipeline achieves an accuracy of up to 99% in recognizing post-surgical and/or post-anesthetic effects based on facial expressions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the pipeline contribution but adds/strengthens a high-accuracy performance result, increasing confidence in the pipeline relative to the preprint description.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The automated system achieved an accuracy of up to 99% for binary 'pain'/'no-pain' classification depending on the treatment. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed semi-automated pipeline achieves an accuracy of up to 99% in recognizing post-surgical and/or post-anesthetic effects based on facial expressions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The performance figure and the role of facial-expression-based classification are reported similarly in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The decision-making process of the neural networks was primarily based on the facial expressions of the mice. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes recognition based on facial expressions but does not explicitly claim that the networks' decision-making was primarily based on them, so the specific interpretability claim is not carried forward.

alignments.pub_claim_text: A new data set of images of black-furred laboratory mice was created for evaluating post-surgical and post-anesthetic effects. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The dataset creation appears as a new, explicit contribution in the publication that was not presented as a distinct claim in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Models trained on datasets with shorter introns consistently outperform those trained on datasets with longer introns. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Models trained on datasets containing shorter introns achieve improved effectiveness for acceptor splice sites, but not for donor splice sites. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restricts the improvement to acceptor sites (excluding donor sites), narrowing and revising the preprint's original generalization about all splice sites.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Models trained with datasets containing multiple introns per sequence demonstrate superior effectiveness over those trained with datasets containing a single intron per sequence. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Models trained on datasets with multiple introns per sequence show higher effectiveness compared to those trained on datasets with a single intron per sequence. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result about multiplicity of introns and improved model effectiveness is consistently reported in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings confirm existing observations from wet lab experiments regarding the impact of length of an intron and the number of introns present in a sequence on splice site prediction effectiveness. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The results contribute to a deeper understanding of how intronic features influence splice site prediction. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the contribution as adding understanding rather than definitively 'confirming' wet-lab observations, softening the strength while keeping the same conceptual link.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Future prediction models should consider factors such as intron length, multiplicity, and the spatial arrangement of splice-related signals. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication includes an explicit recommendation for future models and considerations that was not stated as a distinct claim in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep learning models can accurately predict protein structure from sequence, allowing exploration of protein space from a structural viewpoint. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep learning models can accurately predict protein structure from sequence, allowing exploration of protein space from a structural viewpoint. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The high-level conclusion about accurate structure prediction from sequence is retained verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ABodyBuilder2 was able to predict a small number of 'new' canonical clusters composed of heterogeneous sequences united by a common sequence motif and loop conformation. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ABodyBuilder2 predicted a small number of 'new' canonical clusters composed of heterogeneous sequences united by a common sequence motif and loop conformation. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The reported prediction of novel canonical clusters by ABodyBuilder2 appears unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Models retrained while withholding all antibody structures of a specific CDR loop length or canonical form showed evidence of generalization across CDRs of different lengths. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Models retrained while withholding all antibody structures of a specific CDR loop length showed evidence of generalisation across CDRs of different lengths. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication drops the explicit mention of withholding by canonical form, keeping only withholding by loop length; this narrows the original preprint claim about the withheld categories.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep learning protein structure prediction methods are unable to make completely out-of-domain predictions for CDR loops. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep learning protein structure prediction methods are unable to make completely out-of-domain predictions for CDR loops. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The substantive claim is the same but the publication expresses it with higher certainty than the preprint, representing an amplification of confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Even minimal amounts of data of a structural shape allow the method to recover its original predictive abilities. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Even minimal amounts of data of a structural shape allow the method to recover its original predictive abilities. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The claim about recovery of predictive ability with minimal structural examples is unchanged in content but reported with greater certainty in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our best model achieves 91.7% accuracy on test cameras not seen during training. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We achieve 91.7% accuracy on test cameras not seen during training when classifying micrometeorological conditions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same numeric accuracy on unseen test cameras for the same task with no substantive change in meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We show how effective accuracy is raised to 96% by disregarding 14.1% of classifications where ensemble member models did not reach a consensus. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We show how effective accuracy is raised to 96% by disregarding 14.1% of classifications where ensemble member models did not reach a consensus. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this exact ensemble-consensus filtering result and numbers with no change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We leverage micrometeorological data to demonstrate that experimental warming using open-top chambers shortens early snow events in autumn. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We demonstrate that experimental warming using open-top chambers shortens early snow events in autumn. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same experimental warming effect on early autumn snow events; the method phrasing is slightly condensed but the conclusion is the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Image-derived sunshine marginally outperforms sensor-derived temperature when predicting bumblebee foraging. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Image-derived sunshine marginally outperforms sensor-derived temperature when predicting bumblebee foraging. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication contains the same comparative predictive finding with identical wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Resulting micrometeorological data illustrated common seasonal patterns of summer hailstorms and autumn snowfalls across mountains in the northern and southern hemispheres. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: No publication claim corresponds to the preprint's statement about broad seasonal hailstorm/snowfall patterns; that claim appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Animal sensory systems are more sensitive to common features in the environment than uncommon features. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Human sensory systems are more sensitive to common features in the environment than uncommon features. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication narrows the subject from 'animal' to 'human', changing the claim's scope while keeping the effect and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Artificial neural networks trained to recognize objects have patterns of sensitivity that match the statistics of features in images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Artificial neural networks trained to recognize objects have patterns of sensitivity that match the statistics of features in images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this result unchanged in content and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Learning with gradient descent in deep neural networks preferentially creates representations that are more sensitive to common features. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Learning with gradient descent in neural networks preferentially creates representations that are more sensitive to common features. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same conclusion (minor wording change from 'deep neural networks' to 'neural networks' without altering meaning).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Efficient coding naturally emerges from gradient-like learning on natural stimuli. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Efficient codes can naturally emerge from gradient-like learning. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the idea slightly more cautiously ('can emerge') and recasts wording; this is a clarification with somewhat tempered certainty in phrasing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep learning model predicts four categories of mental health quality using static functional network connectivity (sFNC) data from resting-state fMRI (rs-fMRI). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep learning model predicts four categories of mental health quality using static functional network connectivity data from rs-fMRI. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Direct restatement of the model's prediction capability using sFNC from rs-fMRI.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed model achieved a classification accuracy of 76%, 78%, 88%, and 98% for the excellent, good, fair, and poor mental health categories, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed model achieved classification accuracies of 76%, 78%, 88%, and 98% for excellent, good, fair, and poor mental health categories, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same category-wise accuracy numbers with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Distinct sFNC patterns were identified across each mental health group, with excellent mental health associated with cerebellar-subcortical regions and poor mental health linked to sensorimotor and visual domains. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Distinct static functional network connectivity patterns are associated with different mental health categories, with excellent mental health linked to cerebellar-subcortical regions and poor mental health to sensorimotor and visual domains. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same mapping between sFNC patterns and mental health categories, with equivalent regional attributions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The approach combining rs-fMRI and deep learning has the potential to guide the development of personalized interventions and enable the monitoring of treatment response. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The combination of resting-state fMRI and deep learning has the potential to guide the development of personalized interventions and enable the monitoring of treatment response. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication repeats the same potential/implication claim without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We have developed improved GAN-based approaches for learning models of cell organization. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We have developed improved Generative Adversarial Networks-based approaches for learning models of cell organization. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this methodological contribution verbatim (expanded acronym).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our work provides the first demonstration that, at least for some organelles, deep learning models can capture object-level properties of cell images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our work provides the first demonstration that, at least for some organelles, deep learning models can capture object-level properties of cell images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication contains the same novelty/result claim with no change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We developed novel criteria for evaluating how well synthetic cell images reflect the properties of real images. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We developed novel criteria for evaluating how well synthetic cell images reflect the properties of real images. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the contribution identically.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The first set of criteria measure how well models preserve the expected property that organelles do not overlap. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The first set of criteria measure how well models preserve the expected property that organelles do not overlap. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Direct match; the publication repeats this evaluation criterion and result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A severity index (SI) was defined to quantitatively measure the progression of Alzheimer's disease based on a trajectory obtained from deep learning analysis. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Severity indexes were found to be very closely correlated with all Alzheimer's disease neuropathology biomarkers (R ∼ 0.5, P < 1e-11) and global cognitive function (R = -0.68, P < 2.2e-16). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly restate the procedural definition of the SI but reports results using severity indexes, so the definitional claim is presented implicitly/clarified by downstream results.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Severity indices were found to be very closely correlated with all Alzheimer's disease neuropathology biomarkers and global cognitive function. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Severity indexes were found to be very closely correlated with all Alzheimer's disease neuropathology biomarkers (R ∼ 0.5, P < 1e-11) and global cognitive function (R = -0.68, P < 2.2e-16). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides the same correlation finding, adding precise statistics but not changing the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep learning model remained significantly predictive of neuropathology and clinical severity when applied to additional transcriptomic datasets from different brain regions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep learning model applied to additional transcriptomic data sets from different brain regions remained significantly predictive (P < 1e-3) of neuropathology and clinical severity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms the same cross-dataset predictive performance and reports statistical significance, consistent with the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study resolved four discrete gene modules implicated in vascular and metabolic dysfunction in different cell types. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The integrated index genes resolved four discrete gene modules that are implicated in vascular and metabolic dysfunction in different cell types. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same gene-module resolution result with similar framing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The work demonstrates the generalizability of the transcriptomic signature to various brain donors with Alzheimer's disease and other age-related neurological disorders. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study demonstrates the generalizability of the transcriptomic signature to frontal and temporal cortex measurements and additional brain donors with Alzheimer's disease. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains a generalizability claim but narrows it to specific brain regions and to Alzheimer's disease donors, removing the broader 'other age-related neurological disorders' phrasing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep learning approaches can efficiently replace the manual feature engineering process in the analysis of physiological signals.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep learning architectures can efficiently replace the manual feature engineering process for assessing physiological channels related to nociceptive pain.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the replacement-of-feature-engineering claim but specifies the context ('physiological channels related to nociceptive pain'), making the claim more specific rather than fundamentally different.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed deep learning architectures for assessing physiological channels achieve competitive pain intensity classification without the need for domain-specific expert knowledge.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed uni-modal architecture for electrodermal activity and deep fusion approaches significantly outperform previous methods, achieving average performances of 84.57% and 84.40% respectively.
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the performance/architecture claim but omits the explicit claim about operating without domain-specific expert knowledge and reframes the claim around specific uni-modal/fusion results, altering emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed uni-modal architecture for electrodermal activity and deep fusion approaches significantly outperform previous classification methods, achieving average performances of 85.03% and 83.76%, respectively.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed uni-modal architecture for electrodermal activity and deep fusion approaches significantly outperform previous methods, achieving average performances of 84.57% and 84.40% respectively.
alignments.rationale: The publication makes the same performance claim but reports slightly different average performance values, which is a numerical clarification rather than a conceptual change.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed deep learning approaches offer more flexibility in transfer learning due to the modular nature of deep neural networks.
alignments.rationale: This publication-level claim about transfer-learning flexibility does not appear in the preprint and represents a newly stated benefit.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study delineates the full spectrum of circulating immune cell activation underlying inflammatory processes during infection, immune-mediated inflammatory diseases, and cancer.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study presents a single-cell atlas of more than 6.5 million peripheral blood mononuclear cells from 1,047 patients, which provides a comprehensive model of inflammation in circulating immune cells.
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the 'full spectrum' claim into a concrete, larger-data atlas and explicit comprehensive model, adding dataset details and clarifying scope without negating the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The single-cell atlas of over 2 million peripheral blood mononuclear cells expanded current knowledge of the biology of inflammation in both acute and chronic disease processes.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The atlas expands current knowledge of the biology of inflammation in immune-mediated diseases, acute and chronic inflammatory diseases, infections, and solid tumors.
alignments.rationale: The publication expands the list of disease contexts and (implicitly) the dataset scale, broadening the claimed applicability compared with the preprint statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The research lays the groundwork for developing precision medicine diagnostic tools for patients experiencing severe acute or chronic inflammation.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The research lays the foundation to develop a disease classification framework using unsupervised and explainable machine learning.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the translational intent but specifies the methodological approach (unsupervised, explainable ML) rather than the more general 'precision diagnostics' phrasing in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study presents cells in circulation as a powerful resource for patient stratification in inflammatory diseases.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings present cells in circulation as living biomarkers for inflammatory diseases.
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the utility of circulating cells as 'living biomarkers', a closely related phrasing emphasizing diagnostic/biomarker use rather than the broader 'patient stratification' language.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The study charts altered activity of inflammatory molecules in peripheral blood cells, depicting discriminative inflammation-related genes to further understand mechanisms of inflammation.
alignments.rationale: This explicit claim about charting molecule activity and discriminative genes is not stated in the preprint claims and appears as a new, more detailed result in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The LightGBM classifiers utilizing embeddings from the ESM2 model demonstrate performance gains of 3 - 5% over all compared models for various evaluation metrics.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The LightGBM classifiers utilizing embeddings from the ESM2 model demonstrate performance gains of 3-5% compared to all compared models for various evaluation metrics.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same quantitative performance claim with effectively identical wording and metrics.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The TRILL platform effectively democratizes the usage of open protein language models for predicting crystallization propensities of proteins.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The TRILL platform effectively benchmarks the power of open protein language models for predicting crystallization propensities of proteins.
alignments.rationale: The preprint emphasized democratization/accessibility while the publication emphasizes benchmarking capabilities; the functional emphasis shifts, representing a revision and a narrowing of the originally stated contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A set of 5 novel proteins identified through filtration processes are potentially crystallizable.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A set of 5 novel proteins identified through filtration processes are potentially crystallizable.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this specific result verbatim, with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study benchmarks the power of open protein language models by comparing their performance with state-of-the-art sequence-based methods.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The TRILL platform effectively benchmarks the power of open protein language models for predicting crystallization propensities of proteins.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the benchmarking conclusion but shifts the emphasis onto the TRILL platform as the benchmarking vehicle, clarifying the mechanism behind the comparison.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: TransFew is a new transformer model designed to predict the function of proteins by learning representations of protein sequences and function labels.
alignments.pub_claim_text: TransFew is a new transformer model that predicts protein function by learning representations of protein sequences and function labels.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the contribution identically, with no substantive change in meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: TransFew enhances overall function prediction accuracy compared to existing methods.
alignments.pub_claim_text: TransFew leverages a large pre-trained protein language model and a biological natural language model to enhance function prediction accuracy.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the performance-improvement claim but clarifies the mechanism (leveraging specific pre-trained models) that underlies the accuracy gains.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: TransFew substantially improves the accuracy of predicting rare function terms with limited annotations by facilitating annotation transfer between GO terms.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model demonstrates robust performance in predicting rare function terms with limited annotations by facilitating annotation transfer between GO terms.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this result/claim in equivalent terms, indicating consistent messaging about rare-term performance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep learning models have yielded unprecedented insight into the enhancer code, reaching a level of understanding that may be close to complete.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not make the broad, near-complete-understanding claim; instead it focuses on demonstrable design and analysis results, so the grand, finalizing claim was removed or substantially toned down.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We successfully designed synthetic, cell type specific enhancers using three different design strategies based on deep learning models.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep learning models can be used to efficiently design synthetic, cell-type-specific enhancers from random sequences.
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms successful design of cell-type-specific synthetic enhancers but frames it in terms of designing from random sequences; it does not emphasize the exact 'three strategies' wording, so the claim is clarified and somewhat reframed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The enhancer code can be accurately defined as the optimal strength, combination, and relative distance of transcription factor activator motifs, along with the absence of transcription factor repressor motifs.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The optimization process of enhancer design allows detailed tracing of enhancer features at single-nucleotide resolution.
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes empirical tracing/optimization at high resolution rather than stating a definitive, prescriptive 'code' in the categorical terms given in the preprint, representing a revision and a narrowing/tempering of the original theoretical claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Enhancer design guided by deep learning leads to a better understanding of how enhancers work and shows that their code can be exploited to manipulate cell states.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Synthetic enhancers can specifically target Kenyon cells or glial cells in the fruit fly brain.
alignments.rationale: The publication provides concrete demonstrations (cell-type targeting) that support the preprint's claim about manipulating cell states, clarifying the functional outcomes with specific examples.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The optimization process of enhancer design allows detailed tracing of enhancer features at single-nucleotide resolution.
alignments.rationale: This explicit emphasis on single-nucleotide-resolution tracing of enhancer features is presented as an articulated result in the publication but was not separately stated as a claim in the preprint.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Human enhancers designed using the same strategies adhere to enhancer rules similar to those of Drosophila enhancers.
alignments.rationale: The cross-species claim about human enhancer rules is new in the publication and does not appear among the preprint claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: EvoAug substantially improves the generalization and interpretability of established DNNs across prominent regulatory genomics prediction tasks. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: EvoAug substantially improves the generalization and interpretability of established DNNs across prominent regulatory genomics prediction tasks. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Text is essentially identical between preprint and publication, so the claim is unchanged in meaning, scope, and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The generalization capability of DNNs may be limited by the amount of available data. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not restate this general limitation about data amount; no close corresponding claim appears, so it was omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Random transformation of DNA sequences can potentially alter their function in unknown ways. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Random transformation of DNA sequences can potentially alter their function in unknown ways. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The limitation is carried over verbatim into the publication with the same content and confidence.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed EvoAug suite enhances the training of genomic DNNs by increasing genetic variation. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: This explicit claim about increasing genetic variation in training is present in the publication but not in the preprint, so it is an added specific contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Neuronal responses in an in vitro neuronal network are consistent with ideal Bayesian observer responses under the free energy principle. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Neuronal responses in an in vitro neuronal network are consistent with ideal Bayesian observer responses under the free energy principle. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Claim text and meaning are the same in preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study demonstrates that recorded neuronal responses can be accounted for by variational message passing based on free energy minimisation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study demonstrates that recorded neuronal responses can be accounted for by variational message passing based upon free energy minimisation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result with equivalent wording and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The research provides evidence for functional specialisation and segregation in the in vitro neural network that reproduces in silico learning via free energy minimisation. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The research provides evidence for functional specialisation and segregation in the in vitro neural network that reproduces in silico learning via free energy minimisation. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The claim is carried over without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The combination of in vitro and in silico results characterises learning in terms of trajectories in a variational information plane of accuracy and complexity. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The combination of in vitro and in silico results characterises learning in terms of trajectories in a variational information plane of accuracy and complexity. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This conceptual conclusion is repeated verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: MITRE is the first machine learning method specifically designed for predicting host status from microbiome time-series data. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: MITRE is a supervised machine learning method for microbiome time-series analysis that infers human-interpretable rules linking changes in abundance of clades of microbes to host status. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication removes the strong novelty claim of being "the first" and reframes MITRE as a supervised method that infers interpretable rules, narrowing and tempering the original contribution wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: MITRE maintains interpretability by learning predictive rules over automatically inferred time-periods and phylogenetically related microbes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: MITRE is a supervised machine learning method for microbiome time-series analysis that infers human-interpretable rules linking changes in abundance of clades of microbes to host status. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes the interpretability via inferred human-interpretable rules but omits some specific mechanisms (automatic time-period inference, phylogenetic detail), so the interpretation is clarified and slightly narrowed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: MITRE performs on par or outperforms 'black box' machine learning approaches. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: MITRE performs on par or outperforms conventional difficult-to-interpret machine learning approaches. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The performance claim is preserved with equivalent meaning and confidence in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: MITRE provides a powerful new tool enabling discovery of biologically interpretable relationships between microbiome and human host. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
