alignments.pub_claim_text: MITRE provides a powerful new tool for discovering biologically interpretable relationships between the microbiome and human host. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The core conclusion is preserved, but the publication lowers the expressed certainty from high to medium, so confidence is tempered.

alignments.pub_claim_text: MITRE's performance has been validated on semi-synthetic data and five real datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication includes an explicit validation claim with dataset details that does not appear in the preprint, so this is an added empirical result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: An effective neural network structure rapidly evolves, leading to adaptive inborn behaviour in static environments. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: An effective neural network structure rapidly evolves, leading to adaptive inborn behaviour in static environments. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this primary result with equivalent wording and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The evolutionary outcome depends strongly on the type of social learning and the order of individual and social learning. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The evolutionary outcome depends strongly on the type of social learning (social guidance versus social instruction) and the order of individual and social learning. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds specific examples of social learning types (guidance vs instruction), clarifying rather than changing the core result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The evolutionary dynamics of social learning can be surprisingly complex, with replicate simulations converging to alternative outcomes. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The evolutionary dynamics of social learning can be surprisingly complex, with replicate simulations converging to alternative outcomes. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This claim is carried over verbatim into the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The modelling framework allows the investigation of the interplay between various forms of individual and social learning. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The modelling framework allows the investigation of the interplay between various forms of individual and social learning. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The contribution claim remains the same between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Segregation-to-Integration Transformation (SIT) Model provides a unified account of how the representational structure of a memory is transformed over time. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Segregation-to-Integration Transformation (SIT) model provides a unified account of how the representational structure of a memory is transformed over time. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same overarching account; wording is equivalent and intent unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Memories initially adopt a highly modular or segregated network structure, which serves as an optimal storage buffer. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Memories initially adopt a highly modular or segregated network structure, which serves as an optimal storage buffer. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result is presented identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Over time, neural network reactivations and synaptic plasticity transform the initial modular memory structure into an integrated memory form. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Over time, neural network reactivations and synaptic plasticity transform the initial modular structure into an integrated memory form. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This transformation result is preserved verbatim in the published version.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The SIT Model reveals an optimal window during the transformation of memories where they are most susceptible to malleability. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The SIT model identifies a nonlinear or inverted U-shaped function in memory evolution, indicating that memories are most susceptible to changing their representation early during the transformation. (certainty=medium, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results of the SIT Model integrate a wide range of experimental phenomena related to memory consolidation and reconsolidation. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Segregation-to-Integration Transformation (SIT) model provides a unified account of how the representational structure of a memory is transformed over time. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the model as a unified account that implicitly integrates empirical phenomena; this reframes the original integration claim with slightly different emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study demonstrates that TCR libraries can be effectively panned against the NY-ESO-1 epitope to collect thousands of epitope-specific TCR sequences. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study built large phage display libraries of TCRs with randomized complementary determining region 3 of the β chain targeting the NY-ESO-1 epitope. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the preprint's claim by specifying phage display libraries and randomized CDR3β; the core claim about generating large epitope-targeting TCR libraries is preserved but more explicit in methods.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning TCR-epitope interaction predictor trained on the collected data can identify several epitope-specific TCRs directly from TCR repertoires. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The work demonstrates how display technologies combined with TCR-epitope interaction predictors can effectively leverage large TCR repertoires for TCR discovery. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication states the same capability in broader, more integrative terms (demonstration of approach for discovery) rather than the preprint's specific phrasing, clarifying presentation without changing meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Predicted TCRs displayed activity towards the NY-ESO-1 epitope and showed no detectable cross-reactivity with self-peptides. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Cellular assays revealed that the predicted TCRs displayed activity toward NY-ESO-1 and no detectable cross-reactivity. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The work indicates that combining display technologies with machine learning models can effectively leverage large TCR repertoires for TCR discovery. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The work demonstrates how display technologies combined with TCR-epitope interaction predictors can effectively leverage large TCR repertoires for TCR discovery. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication states the same conclusion but with stronger certainty and framing as a demonstrated method, so the claim is unchanged but confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The automated detection algorithm showed state-of-the-art performance for detecting epileptic activity from clinical EEG, with mean sensitivity of >95% and a mean false positive rate of 1 detection per minute. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The automated detection algorithm showed state-of-the-art performance for detecting epileptic activity from clinical EEG, with mean sensitivity of >95% and a mean false positive rate of 1 detection per minute. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The automated detection algorithm reduced human review time by 80%-99%, without compromising event detection or diagnostic accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical claim in preprint and publication about review time reduction and retained accuracy.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Computer-assisted review can increase the speed and accuracy of EEG assessment and has the potential to greatly improve therapeutic outcomes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Computer-assisted review can increase the speed and accuracy of EEG assessment and has the potential to greatly improve therapeutic outcomes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The concluding statement is carried over verbatim between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed CNN approach for estimating tree defoliation from ground-level RGB photos achieves an average mean absolute error of 7.6% after cross-validation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed CNN approach for estimating tree defoliation from ground-level RGB photos achieves an average mean absolute error (avgMAE) of 7.6% after cross-validation. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The CNN approach performs only 0.9% points worse than a group of human experts on one of the data sets. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Tree defoliation estimation from ground-level RGB images using CNNs works well and achieves performance close to that of human experts. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Tree defoliation estimation from ground-level RGB images with a CNN works well and achieves performance close to human experts. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is restated equivalently in the publication, preserving scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The physics-based machine learning approach can predict in vitro transcription factor binding affinities from structural and mechanical DNA properties. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The physics-based machine learning approach predicts in vitro transcription factor binding affinities from structural and mechanical DNA properties derived from atomistic molecular dynamics simulations. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies that the structural/mechanical properties are derived from atomistic MD simulations, clarifying the source of inputs while preserving the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method demonstrates excellent performance in predicting affinities compared to existing algorithms. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The method demonstrates excellent performance in predicting affinities compared to existing algorithms. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The performance comparison claim is unchanged between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method can be extended to account for epigenetic variants, mismatches, mutations, or any non-coding nucleobases. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The method can be extended to epigenetic variants, mismatches, mutations, or any non-coding nucleobases. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the stated extensibility of the method with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: When complemented with chromatin structure information, the method provides good estimates of in vivo binding sites in yeast. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: When complemented with chromatin structure information, the in vitro trained method provides good estimates of in vivo binding sites in yeast. (certainty=medium, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PyGenePlexus enables users to gain insight into any gene set of interest based on a molecular interaction network using supervised machine learning. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: PyGenePlexus provides predictions of how associated every gene in the network is to the input gene set. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PyGenePlexus provides predictions of how associated every gene in the network is to the input gene set. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: PyGenePlexus provides predictions of how associated every gene in the network is to the input gene set. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PyGenePlexus offers interpretability by comparing the model trained on the input gene set to models trained on thousands of known gene sets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: PyGenePlexus offers interpretability by comparing the model trained on the input gene set to models trained on thousands of known gene sets. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PyGenePlexus returns the network connectivity of the top predicted genes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: PyGenePlexus returns the network connectivity of the top predicted genes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains this functional result without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed artificial neural network (ANN) incorporates oscillatory dynamics to convert simultaneous inputs into a temporal code.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our model implements the idea that inhibitory oscillations segregate competing inputs in time.
alignments.rationale: Both claims express the same core idea (oscillatory/inhibitory oscillations temporally segregate competing inputs); the publication frames it more generally as implementing this idea, so I mark it as a clarification.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Without the introduced temporal dynamics, the trained network produced a mixed output when presented with two letters simultaneously, indicating a bottleneck problem.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The trained network correctly classified individual letters but produced a mixed output when presented with two letters simultaneously, indicating a bottleneck problem.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result (mixed output / bottleneck) with essentially identical meaning and emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The output nodes of the network activated sequentially along the phase of the inhibitory oscillations when refraction and oscillatory inhibition were introduced.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Introducing refraction and oscillatory inhibition allows the output nodes corresponding to two stimuli to activate sequentially, ordered along the phase of the inhibitory oscillations.
alignments.rationale: The publication claim mirrors the preprint result about sequential activation ordered by inhibitory phase with no substantive change in meaning or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model provides experimentally testable predictions regarding how the primate visual system handles competing stimuli.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not assert explicit experimentally testable predictions for the primate visual system; instead it focuses on implementing the segregation idea and adding an applications-oriented claim, so the specific preprint claim is effectively dropped.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The neural networks designed with properties of the subcortical pathway could classify seven facial expressions with a correct rate of 51%.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Neural networks constructed with properties of the subcortical pathway achieved modest accuracy in classifying facial expressions.
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses the same result more qualitatively as "modest accuracy" rather than the specific 51% figure; this is a clarification accompanied by a slight tempering of confidence/precision.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The performance of the neural networks improved when the properties of the subcortical pathway were replaced with corresponding features from the cortical pathway.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Replacing subcortical properties with cortical features gradually improved performance in facial expression classification.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this finding directly (performance improves when subcortical properties are replaced by cortical features) with equivalent meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: All three properties of the subcortical pathway constrain the accuracy of facial expression information.
alignments.pub_claim_text: All three properties of the subcortical pathway limit the accuracy of facial expression information.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the conclusion but states it with higher asserted certainty (publication label is high certainty), so I mark confidence as amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Some processing units in the final layer of the neural networks were sensitive to spatial frequencies in both retina-based and object-based coordinates.
alignments.rationale: The publication's listed claims do not include the specific observation about final-layer units' coordinate-frame sensitivity, so this specific result appears omitted from the published claim set.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The simple and population synergy models emulate the robustness of muscle synergies against cortical stroke observed in human stroke patients.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The simple and population synergy models emulate the robustness of muscle synergies against cortical stroke observed in human stroke patients.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this result verbatim; no substantive change detected.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The size of the spinal variation of the population synergy matched well with the variation in spinal PreM-INs recorded in monkeys.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The size of the spinal variation of the population synergy matched well with the variation in spinal PreM-INs recorded in monkeys.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this empirical match directly and without change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A spinal population with moderate variation is a biologically plausible model for the neural implementation of muscle synergies.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A spinal population with moderate variation is a biologically plausible model for the neural implementation of muscle synergies.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves this conclusion exactly, with equivalent framing and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Third-order, or triple, correlation fully characterizes neural network activity using the triple correlation uniqueness (TCU) theorem.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Third-order (triple) correlation fully characterizes neural network activity using the triple correlation uniqueness theorem.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Classifying triplet motifs by their event sequencing leads to fourteen qualitatively distinct motif classes that represent well-studied network behaviors.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Classifying triplet motifs by their event sequencing leads to fourteen qualitatively distinct motif classes that embody well-studied network behaviors.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The summed triple correlations within motif classes provide novel metrics of network behavior and include commonly used analyses.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The summed triple correlations provide novel metrics of network behavior and are inclusive of commonly used analyses.
alignments.rationale: The publication states the same methodological claim but with higher-certainty framing (publication lists it as a high-certainty result), so confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This approach can be easily applied to any recording modality, making existing neural datasets suitable for reanalysis.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This approach can be easily applied to any recording modality, making existing neural datasets ripe for reanalysis.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the contribution claim about broad applicability and reanalysis potential with equivalent meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Triple correlation is an accessible signal processing tool with a solid theoretical foundation capable of revealing previously elusive information within recordings of neural networks.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Triple correlation is an accessible signal processing tool capable of revealing previously elusive information within recordings of neural networks.
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same conclusion but with a somewhat lower certainty label (medium vs preprint high), so I mark the confidence as tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The optimization of an anatomically-constrained shallow neural network model arrives at two distinct, opposing solutions for detecting impending collisions.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The optimization of anatomically-constrained shallow neural network models arrives at two distinct solutions for detecting impending collisions, only one of which matches the actual dendritic weighting of LPLC2 neurons.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Only one of the solutions from the neural network optimization matches the actual dendritic weighting of LPLC2 neurons.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The optimization of anatomically-constrained shallow neural network models arrives at two distinct solutions for detecting impending collisions, only one of which matches the actual dendritic weighting of LPLC2 neurons.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The trained model reproduces experimentally observed LPLC2 neuron responses for many stimuli and canonical tuning of loom sensitive neurons.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LPLC2-like solutions are favored when a population of units is trained on the collision detection task, but not when units are trained in isolation.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Both solutions derived from the neural network models can solve the collision detection inference problem with high accuracy when the population size is sufficiently large.
alignments.rationale: The preprint emphasized a preference for LPLC2-like solutions in population training; the publication instead emphasizes that both solutions can work when population size is large, which revises and broadens the earlier, more selective claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Rotation-Chip achieves a high fluid transport efficiency of 92.78% without observable leaking. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same finding about seven subtypes and their distinct cellular profiles with no substantive change in meaning or scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Atypical subpopulations of TNBC patients with moderate prognosis were identified, marked by GATA3 over-expression. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We reveal atypical subpopulations of triple-negative breast cancer patients with moderate prognosis and Luminal A patients with poor prognosis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the discovery of atypical TNBC subpopulations with moderate prognosis but omits the specific marker (GATA3) in the concise claim, changing details of the preprint claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Luminal A patients with poor prognosis were identified, marked by KRT6 and ACTA2 over-expression and CDH1 under-expression. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We reveal atypical subpopulations of triple-negative breast cancer patients with moderate prognosis and Luminal A patients with poor prognosis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the identification of a poor-prognosis Luminal A subpopulation but omits the specific gene-expression markers reported in the preprint, representing a revision in detail.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study provides important guidelines on bridging single-cell level information towards population-level survival prediction. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly state the methodological guideline/conclusion about bridging single-cell to population-level prediction present in the preprint; that specific conclusion is not carried forward.

alignments.pub_claim_text: These atypical subpopulations are validated in TCGA-BRCA and METABRIC datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The validation in TCGA and METABRIC appears as a new, explicit claim in the publication that was not present in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Metal3D is the most accurate metal ion location predictor to date, outperforming geometric predictors including Metal1D by a wide margin. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Metal3D is the most accurate zinc ion location predictor to date with predictions within 0.70 ± 0.64 Å of experimental locations. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the 'most accurate' claim but narrows it from generic metal ions to zinc specifically, changing the original broader claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The predicted metal ion locations for Metal3D are within 0.70 ± 0.64 Å of the experimental locations, with half of the sites below 0.5 Å. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Metal3D is the most accurate zinc ion location predictor to date with predictions within 0.70 ± 0.64 Å of experimental locations. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numerical accuracy statement is reproduced exactly for prediction error, matched in the publication (now specified for zinc).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Metal3D outputs a confidence metric for each predicted site and works on proteins with few homologues in the protein data bank. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Metal3D outputs a confidence metric for each predicted site and works on proteins with few homologes in the protein data bank. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this capability verbatim but with higher stated certainty (result vs. conclusion), effectively amplifying confidence in the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Metal3D predicts a global metal density that can be used for annotation of structures predicted using AlphaFold2. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Metal3D predicts a global zinc density that can be used for annotation of computationally predicted structures. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the global density prediction claim but clarifies it as zinc-specific and broadens 'AlphaFold2' to 'computationally predicted structures'—overall a slight narrowing to zinc with minor rephrasing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Metal3D can be used in protein design workflows for the location of suitable metal binding sites and rotamer sampling to create novel metalloproteins. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly claim application in protein design workflows as stated in the preprint; that proposed usage is not asserted in the published claims.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The framework of Metal3D is readily extensible to other metals by modifying the training data. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication introduces an explicit claim about extensibility to other metals that was not present in the preprint claims list.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: EARBOX is a low-cost, open-source system for automated phenotyping of maize ears. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: EARBOX is a low-cost, open-source system for automated phenotyping of maize ears. (certainty=high, type=contribution)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The imaging platform of EARBOX captures a range of trait data per ear, including ear shape, dimensions, and the distribution of grain dimensions along the ear. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: EARBOX provides robust and accurate measurements of maize ear traits, including spatial features. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The system derives novel traits, such as the distribution of grain dimensions along grain cohorts and the distribution of abortion frequency along the ear. (certainty=medium, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: deepFPlearn predicts the association between chemical structures and effects on the gene/pathway level using a combined deep learning approach. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The feature compression in deepFPlearn preserves relevant chemical structural information. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using a vast chemical inventory as input for the autoencoder did not reduce prediction quality but allowed capturing a more comprehensive range of chemical structures. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using a vast chemical inventory as input for the autoencoder did not reduce prediction quality but allowed capturing a more comprehensive range of chemical structures. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.pub_claim_text: deepFPlearn classifies hundreds of thousands of chemicals in seconds. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: deepFPlearn is provided as an open-source and flexible tool that can be easily retrained and customized. (certainty=high, type=contribution)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: An ensemble of Deep Learning architectures consistently achieves peak prediction performance across various interaction types.
alignments.pub_claim_text: An ensemble architecture, which combines all six architectures, consistently achieves peak prediction accuracy for protein interface residue prediction.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The PIPENN sequence-based ensemble predictor outperforms current state-of-the-art sequence-based protein interface predictors on the ZK448 benchmark across all interaction types.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the outperforming claim but clarifies that the comparison is on the ZK448 benchmark, which narrows the claim to that benchmark.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The constructed BioDL data set includes protein-protein interaction data from the PDB and protein-ligand interactions from the BioLip database.
alignments.rationale: The publication's listed claims do not mention the BioDL dataset construction or its sources, indicating this contribution was omitted from the published claims.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The AUC-ROC achieved by PIPENN is 0.718 for protein-protein, 0.823 for protein-nucleotide, and 0.842 for protein-small molecule interactions.
alignments.rationale: The publication includes specific performance metrics (AUC-ROC per interaction type) that were not present in the preprint claims, so this is an added, more detailed result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our method could generate better profiles than existing de novo profile generators, including CSBuild and RPS-BLAST, on the basis of profile-sequence similarity search performance.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our method could generate better profiles than existing de novo profile generators, including CSBuild and RPS-BLAST.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the preprint result essentially verbatim, preserving meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LSTM had high potential power to detect long-range interactions between amino acids, as in the case of beta-strand formation.
alignments.pub_claim_text: LSTM had high potential power to detect long-range interactions between amino acids, as in the case of beta-strand formation.
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about LSTM's potential is restated identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our de novo profile generator, SPBuild, achieved higher performance than that of existing methods for profile prediction of beta-strands.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our de novo profile generator, SPBuild, achieved higher performance than that of existing methods for profile prediction of beta-strands.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the preprint's result without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our findings will be useful for the development of other prediction methods related to biological sequences by machine learning methods.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our findings will be useful for the development of other prediction methods related to biological sequences by machine learning methods.
alignments.rationale: The stated broader usefulness of the findings is preserved in the publication with no material change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our open-source approach integrates machine learning and statistical inference techniques to classify animal behaviour from raw acceleration data.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our approach integrates machine learning and statistical inference techniques to evaluate and mitigate class imbalances, changes in model performance across ecological settings and noisy classifications.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We demonstrate significantly improved predictions in African wild dog behaviour classification, particularly for rare behaviours such as feeding.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We demonstrate significantly improved predictions along with associated uncertainty metrics in African wild dog behaviour classification.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Correct classifications of behaviours more than doubled following quality checks and data rebalancing introduced in our pipeline.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Correct classifications of rare and ecologically important behaviours such as feeding more than doubled following quality checks and data rebalancing introduced in our pipeline.

alignments.pub_claim_text: For DNA CpG methylation state prediction, DeepSignal achieves 90% correlation with bisulfite sequencing using just 20�� coverage of reads. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a specific result about CpG methylation correlation with bisulfite sequencing at 20x coverage that is not present in the preprint.

alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepSignal can predict methylation states of 5% more DNA CpGs that previously cannot be predicted by bisulfite sequencing. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication includes a novel claim about increased CpG coverage relative to bisulfite sequencing that was not stated in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The variational Bayesian microbiome multiomics (VBayesMM) approach enhances the microbe–metabolite vectors (MMvec) method by incorporating a spike-and-slab prior within a Bayesian neural network.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The variational Bayesian microbiome multiomics (VBayesMM) approach improves the prediction of metabolite abundances from microbial metagenomics data.
alignments.rationale: The preprint emphasizes the specific methodological innovation (spike-and-slab prior in a Bayesian neural network) while the publication reframes the contribution in terms of improved prediction of metabolite abundances; this is a clarification and broader framing of impact.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: VBayesMM improves the accuracy of estimated co-occurrence probabilities between microbes and metabolites.
alignments.pub_claim_text: VBayesMM allows for rapid and precise identification of crucial microbial species, leading to more accurate estimations of co-occurrence probabilities between microbes and metabolites.
alignments.rationale: The publication explicitly links rapid/precise identification of species to improved co-occurrence estimates, expanding the mechanism but preserving the core claim of improved accuracy.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our comparative evaluation demonstrates that VBayesMM outperforms existing methods in accuracy and provides a scalable solution for analyzing massive datasets.
alignments.pub_claim_text: VBayesMM outperforms existing methods in predicting metabolite abundances and provides a scalable solution for analyzing massive datasets.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the performance and scalability claim with the same scope (outperforming existing methods and scalability) focused on metabolite prediction.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: VBayesMM enhances the interpretability of the Bayesian neural network by identifying a core set of influential microbial species.
alignments.pub_claim_text: VBayesMM enhances the interpretability of the Bayesian neural network by identifying a core set of influential microbial species.
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same interpretability claim (identifying a core set of influential species) with equivalent wording and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed end-to-end convolutional spiking neural network (SNN) model for visual place recognition (VPR) outperforms state-of-the-art artificial neural networks (ANNs) and SNNs by achieving 78.2% precision at 100% recall on the Nordland dataset.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model achieves 78.6% precision at 100% recall on the Nordland dataset, compared to 73.0% from the current state-of-the-art.
alignments.rationale: The publication refines the numeric result (78.6% vs 78.2%) and places it in direct comparison to prior state-of-the-art, clarifying the original performance claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The performance of the proposed SNN model is competitive on the Oxford RobotCar dataset.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed end-to-end convolutional spiking neural network (SNN) model for visual place recognition (VPR) significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art SNNs on challenging datasets.
alignments.rationale: The preprint hedged 'competitive' on a specific dataset, whereas the publication asserts significant outperformance across challenging datasets, a stronger and broader claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed method is easier to train and faster in both training and inference compared to other ANN and SNN-based methods.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The approach offers a simpler training pipeline while yielding significant improvements in both training and inference times compared to state-of-the-art SNNs for VPR.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the training/inference speed and simplicity claim but narrows the comparison to SNNs (omitting ANNs), clarifying the comparison scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Explicit hierarchical structure fails to provide an advantage for generalization above a flat model that does not incorporate hierarchical structure.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Explicit hierarchical structure by itself fails to provide a critical performance advantage when compared with a 'flat' model that does not incorporate hierarchical structure.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the preprint result verbatim in meaning and strength regarding the lack of advantage from explicit hierarchical structure.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Hierarchy appears to facilitate cognitive control processes that support non-routine behaviors and behaviors that are carried out under computational stress.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Hierarchy appears to facilitate cognitive control processes that support nonroutine behaviors and behaviors that are carried out under computational stress.
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about hierarchy facilitating cognitive control under nonroutine or stressed conditions is preserved unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A model that incorporates wiring costs in the cost function best accounts for fMRI data collected from human participants.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A model that incorporates wiring costs in the cost function best accounts for fMRI data collected from human participants.
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same modeling result attributing best fit to models that include wiring costs.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The anterior cingulate cortex encodes distributed representations of sequential task context along a rostro-caudal gradient of abstraction.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The results reveal that the ACC encodes distributed representations of sequential task context along a rostro-caudal gradient of abstraction.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same neuroimaging result about ACC encoding sequential task context along a rostro-caudal abstraction gradient.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Rostral ACC encodes relatively abstract and temporally extended patterns of activity compared to those encoded by caudal ACC.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Rostral ACC encodes relatively abstract and temporally extended patterns of activity compared with those encoded by caudal ACC.
alignments.rationale: The substantive result is preserved but the publication presents it with higher stated certainty (publication labels it high certainty), so confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The network’s internal representations developed through task-optimization generalize from synthetic data to predict single-trial neural activity in CN and S1 of primates performing center-out reaching.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The emerging, task-optimized internal representations generalize from synthetic data to predict neural dynamics in CN and S1 of primates.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the core generalization claim but uses broader language ('neural dynamics' vs 'single-trial activity' and omits the specific task), clarifying and broadening the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Task-driven models outperform linear encoding models and data-driven models.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly assert a direct outperforming of linear encoding and data-driven models; no clear matching claim is present, so this preprint claim appears removed or unreported.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Behavioral tasks, which aim to predict the limb position and velocity, were the best to predict the neural activity in both areas.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Computational tasks that aim to predict the limb position and velocity were the best at predicting the neural activity in both areas.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the specific finding that limb-position/velocity-predicting tasks yielded the best neural predictions in both areas.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Architectures that are better at solving the tasks are also better at predicting the neural data.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The emerging, task-optimized internal representations generalize from synthetic data to predict neural dynamics in CN and S1 of primates.
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes that task-optimized representations generalize to predict neural data, which clarifies the link between task-solving ability and neural prediction though it does not explicitly frame it as an architecture comparison.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Neural activity in CN and S1 is top-down modulated during goal-directed movements.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Neural activity in the CN and S1 is top-down modulated during goal-directed movements.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the claim but presents it with higher stated confidence (publication marks it medium vs preprint low), so confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The N170 peak in event-related potentials (ERPs) has greater amplitude and shorter latency when elicited by human faces compared to images of other objects.
alignments.rationale: The publication's claims focus on the model and its fit to ERP data rather than reporting this specific empirical N170 result, so the specific ERP finding is not present in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The computational model developed, consisting of a convolutional neural network (CNN) connected to a recurrent neural network (RNN), was trained to reproduce ERP waveforms evoked by visual events.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The computational model developed in this study demonstrates strong correlation with open-access data (r=0.98) and validation study data (r=0.78).
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes the model's empirical fit to data; it does not detail architecture in the Claim text, so the method description from the preprint is reframed as demonstrated model performance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Cross-validation model outputs strongly correlated with open-access data (r = 0.98) and validation study data (r = 0.78).
alignments.pub_claim_text: The computational model developed in this study demonstrates strong correlation with open-access data (r=0.98) and validation study data (r=0.78).
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same quantitative cross-validation correlation results.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Some aspects of the model's behavior were consistent with neural recordings, while others were not, indicating a limited capacity for modeling the neurophysiology of face-sensitive ERP generation.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model's behavior shows some consistency with neural recordings, indicating a promising capacity for modeling the neurophysiology of face-sensitive ERP generation.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the notion of some consistency with neural data but shifts the interpretation from a limitation to emphasizing promising capacity, revising the evaluative stance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: NN-LMM had significantly better prediction performance than the recently proposed single-step approach for genomic prediction with intermediate omics data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: NN-MM had significantly better prediction performance than the recently proposed single-step approach for genomic prediction with intermediate omics data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result is the same in substance (performance advantage of the neural method over the single-step approach); differences are limited to a naming/typo change (NN-LMM vs NN-MM).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: NN-LMM can handle various patterns of missing omics measures. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: NN-MM can handle various patterns of missing omics measures. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about handling missing omics patterns is preserved verbatim aside from the name change, so the claim is effectively unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: NN-LMM allows nonlinear relationships between intermediate omics features and phenotypes. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: NN-MM allows nonlinear relationships between intermediate omics features and phenotypes. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The claim that the method permits nonlinear relationships is carried over directly; only the model name differs.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: NN-LMM incorporates intermediate omics features by adding middle layers between genotypes and phenotypes. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: NN-MM incorporates intermediate omics features by adding middle layers between genotypes and phenotypes. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The methodological contribution (adding middle layers) is identical in the publication, with only the model name differing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Circuit formation based on learning rather than preprogramming can explain some aspects of spinal cord connectivity patterns observed in animals. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Circuit formation based on learning rather than preprogramming can explain the selective formation of monosynaptic projections between muscle spindle primary afferents and homonymous motoneurons. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the general learning-based explanation into a specific, testable case (selective monosynaptic projections), narrowing and clarifying the original general claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The connection matrices resulting from the model support functional self-organization of the mature pattern of Ia to motoneuron connectivity in the spinal circuitry. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Connection matrices emerge that support functional self-organization of the mature pattern of Ia to motoneuron connectivity in the spinal circuitry without explicit need for guiding genetic rules. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the emergence of connection matrices supporting self-organization and adds explicit emphasis that this occurs without guiding genetic rules, a clarifying nuance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: More coordinated muscle activity patterns observed during neonatal locomotion impaired projection selectivity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Stronger and more coordinated muscle activity patterns observed later during neonatal locomotion impaired projection selectivity. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies timing ('later') and strength ('stronger') and reduces certainty from high to medium, clarifying and somewhat narrowing the original statement while tempering confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A musculoskeletal system can imprint important aspects of its mechanical dynamics onto a neural network without specific preprogramming. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings imply a generic functionality of a musculoskeletal system to imprint important aspects of its mechanical dynamics onto a neural network. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses the same conclusion as a generic functionality implied by the findings; the meaning is preserved though phrasing is slightly changed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This functionality may help explain patterns of connectivity and associated reflexes that appear during abnormal development. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not state the hypothesis linking the functionality to abnormal-development connectivity/reflex patterns, so this preprint claim appears omitted in the final paper.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Normal connectivity patterns developed only when β motoneurons were modeled, which were not considered in previous literature regarding selective formation of these synapses. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication includes a specific result about the role of β motoneurons that was not present in the preprint, so this is an added publication claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A rapid untargeted approach for the sensitive detection of microbial contaminants at low abundance from low volume samples during the manufacturing process of cell therapies has been developed. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This research presents a novel method for rapidly and accurately detecting microbial contaminants in cell therapy products. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the central contribution (a rapid, accurate method for detecting contaminants in cell therapy products); wording is slightly more general but meaning is the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed methodology can detect microbial samples at 10 CFU/mL using metagenomic classification. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: By combining advanced long-read nanopore sequencing techniques and machine learning, we can effectively identify the presence and types of microbial contaminants at low abundance levels. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The specific numeric sensitivity (10 CFU/mL) reported in the preprint is not stated in the publication, which instead asserts effective detection at 'low abundance'—the claim was generalized (revised) in the final text.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning can be coupled with long read sequencing to detect and identify sample sterility status and microbial species present in T-cell cultures. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: By combining advanced long-read nanopore sequencing techniques and machine learning, we can effectively identify the presence and types of microbial contaminants at low abundance levels. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the coupling of ML and long-read sequencing as an effective (result-level) method for identifying contaminants; this clarifies and strengthens the preprint's conclusion into a firmer result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This research presents a novel method that can significantly reduce the time required for sterility testing compared to traditional methods, which take 7-14 days. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Traditional testing methods for microbial contaminants take 7-14 days, while our approach can significantly reduce this time. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both preprint and publication state the same comparative time-reduction result relative to traditional 7–14 day tests; wording and certainty are consistent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The breakthrough in detection methods has the potential to improve the safety and efficiency of cell therapy manufacturing. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This breakthrough has the potential to improve the safety and efficiency of cell therapy manufacturing. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The stated potential impact on safety and efficiency is preserved from preprint to publication with equivalent framing and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The analysis distinguished three distinct disease subtypes with highly predictable progression rates corresponding to slow, moderate, and fast disease progression. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The analysis distinguished three distinct disease subtypes with highly predictable progression rates corresponding to slow, moderate, and fast disease progression. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The core result identifying three subtypes with corresponding progression rates is identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The models achieved highly accurate projections of disease progression five years after initial diagnosis with an average area under the curve (AUC) of 0.92. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The models achieved highly accurate projections of disease progression 5 years after initial diagnosis, with an average area under the curve (AUC) of 0.92 for the slower progressing group, 0.87 for moderate progressors, and 0.95 for the fast-progressing group. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication refines the single averaged AUC into group-specific AUCs, clarifying the performance distribution across subtypes and narrowing the original aggregate statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Serum neurofilament light (Nfl) was identified as a significant indicator of fast disease progression among other key biomarkers. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Serum neurofilament light was identified as a significant indicator of fast disease progression among other key biomarkers of interest. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The identification of NfL as an indicator is preserved, but the publication reports a lower certainty level (high→medium), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The data-driven study provides insights to deconstruct Parkinson's disease heterogeneity, which could improve clinical trial outcomes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The data-driven study provides insights to deconstruct Parkinson's disease heterogeneity, which could have immediate implications for clinical trials. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes more immediate implications for trials compared with the preprint's phrasing, clarifying the potential translational impact while retaining similar certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning models are anticipated to improve patient counseling, clinical trial design, allocation of healthcare resources, and individualized patient care. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning models are anticipated to improve patient counseling, clinical trial design, and ultimately individualized patient care. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication shortens the list of anticipated benefits (removing explicit mention of resource allocation), narrows the scope of claimed impacts, and lowers certainty from medium to low.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study achieved an accuracy of 72.7% in classifying known specimens using extreme gradient boosting (XGBoost) at the lowest identified taxonomic level (LITL). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The maximum accuracy achieved by the eXtreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) model was 72.7% when classifying known specimens at the lowest identified taxonomic level (LITL). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numeric accuracy and context (known specimens, LITL, XGBoost) are preserved exactly in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Models trained without contextual metadata underperformed by an average of 7.2% compared to models that included contextual metadata. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Models trained with contextual metadata outperformed those trained without it. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the core finding that metadata improves performance but omits the specific 7.2% figure; the substantive relationship is unchanged though the numeric detail is removed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The overall accuracy across the entire NEON dataset was 71.5%, including classifications of unknown taxa using zero-shot classification. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report the stated overall 71.5% accuracy for the full NEON dataset including zero-shot classifications, so this specific aggregate result appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The methodology presented represents a realistic application of machine learning for ecological studies. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The methodology outlined in the study represents a realistic application of machine learning for ecological data acquisition. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the methodological claim but strengthens it (contribution and higher certainty), so the idea is unchanged but confidence/weight is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The inclusion of contextual metadata can significantly improve the accuracy of machine learning models in ecological data classification. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The inclusion of contextual metadata can provide significant improvements to the accuracy of machine learning models. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same conclusion about metadata improving accuracy but with stronger certainty (medium→high), indicating an amplification of confidence.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The study demonstrates that convolutional neural networks are not necessarily more accurate than traditional machine learning methods. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication introduces a comparative claim about CNNs versus traditional methods that does not appear in the preprint, so this is an added publication claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Meridian can be predicted by machine learning approaches with a top accuracy of 0.83. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Meridian can be predicted by machine learning approaches with a top accuracy of 0.83. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result and identical top accuracy, so the claim is unchanged in meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This is the first time that molecular properties of the herb compounds are associated with the TCM Meridians. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This is the first time that molecular properties of the herb compounds are associated with the TCM Meridians. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the contribution claim verbatim, with no change in scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Meridians of herbs are associated with the properties of the compounds. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We identified the top compound features that were important for the Meridian prediction. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication gives a more specific result (identifying top compound features) which clarifies and narrows the preprint's broader association claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning approach may provide novel insights for the understanding of molecular evidence of Meridians in TCM. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine learning approach may provide novel insights for the understanding of molecular evidence of Meridians in TCM. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The concluding statement is retained verbatim with the same level of certainty and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The wet and dry EEG systems can deliver comparable results despite the latter having one third as many EEG channels as the former. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The dry EEG system can deliver comparable or even better results than the wet EEG system, despite having one third as many EEG channels. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication strengthens the preprint's claim (adds that dry can be 'even better'), so the claim content is revised and confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The new neural-network architecture outperforms the baseline stimulus-reconstruction methods for both EEG modalities. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The new neural-network architecture outperforms the baseline linear stimulus-reconstruction method, improving decoding accuracy from 66% to 81% using wet EEG. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication quantifies the improvement for wet EEG, clarifying the preprint's general outperforming claim and presenting stronger numeric evidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The new neural-network architecture outperforms the baseline stimulus-reconstruction methods for both EEG modalities. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The new neural-network architecture improves decoding accuracy from 59% to 87% for dry EEG. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides numerical improvement for dry EEG as well, clarifying and strengthening the preprint's modality-agnostic outperforming claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study provides a 14-subject, wet-electrode AAD dataset and an 11-subject, dry-electrode AAD dataset for further validation and experimentation. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study provides datasets and software for further validation, experimentation, and modification in auditory attention decoding research. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the dataset contribution but emphasizes availability of datasets and software without restating the exact subject counts, broadening the contribution description.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Neurons tuned to numbers can arise in random feedforward networks, even in the complete absence of learning. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Number-selective neurons can arise spontaneously in an artificial deep neural network without any learning. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the core result that number-selective neurons arise without learning in similar terms, keeping meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Number tuning arises in networks trained to non-numerical natural images, randomized after training, and those never trained. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The responses of these neurons can induce the abstract number sense, allowing discrimination of numerosity independent of low-level visual cues. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes the functional consequence (abstract number sense independent of low-level cues) which clarifies the significance of the preprint's observation across training conditions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Number-tuned neurons showed characteristics observed in the brain following the Weber-Fechner law. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The responses of number-selective neurons in the network exhibit characteristics similar to those observed in the brain. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the similarity-to-brain result but omits the explicit Weber–Fechner formulation, revising the specific framing of the comparison.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Number-tuned neurons suddenly vanished when the feedforward weight variation decreased to a certain level. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report a claim about sudden vanishing of tuned neurons with decreased weight variation, so this specific result is not present.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Number tuning can develop from the statistical variation of bottom-up projections in the visual pathway, initializing innate number sense. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: These findings provide insight into the origin of innate cognitive functions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication generalizes the implication to innate cognitive functions, broadening the preprint's conclusion about number sense while preserving the causal insight.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Significant progress has been made in automated neuron reconstruction and detection of synapses. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication focuses on CMN capabilities and does not include the broad general statement about overall field progress, so the general claim is not present.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Cellular morphology neural networks (CMNs) were introduced to analyze nanometer-resolution reconstructions of brain tissue. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CMNs can identify subcellular compartments and the cell types of neuron reconstructions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents concrete uses of CMNs for analyzing reconstructions (compartments and cell types), which clarifies and operationalizes the preprint's contribution statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CMNs can identify glia cells and resolve neuron reconstruction errors. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Cellular morphology neural networks (CMNs) can identify glia cells in a supervised classification paradigm. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication explicitly reports glia identification by CMNs, matching the preprint claim directly.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CMNs can identify glia cells and resolve neuron reconstruction errors. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CMNs can be used to resolve neuron reconstruction errors. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication explicitly reports that CMNs resolve reconstruction errors, directly matching the preprint's second part of the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CMNs can be used to identify subcellular compartments and the cell types of neuron reconstructions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CMNs can identify subcellular compartments and the cell types of neuron reconstructions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This claim is present verbatim in the publication, so it is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep learning model achieved a precision of 0.987 for Polypedates leucomystax and 1 for Rhinella marina when evaluated with wild sounds from Ishigaki Island. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Model precision and recall for wild sounds were 0.972 and 0.906 for P. leucomystax, and 0.899 and 0.705 for R. marina, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports different (lower) precision values for wild sounds, so the numeric result has been revised and the claim is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Model recall values decreased for playback sounds, with 0.629 for Polypedates leucomystax and 0.906 for Rhinella marina. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Playback survey dates were identifiable from the high number of detections when the detection thresholds were properly adjusted. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports playback-related findings but frames them differently (detectability of survey dates rather than the specific recall numbers reported in the preprint), so the playback recall claim is revised and less directly supported.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The training data from Ishigaki Island allowed the model to generalize adequately across the invasion front, despite the absence of local training data from Iriomote Island. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Data from Ishigaki Island enabled training a model with adequate generalization across the invasion front. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the conclusion that Ishigaki data allowed adequate generalization across the invasion front, matching the preprint's conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This work demonstrates analysis of bacterial abundance from three contrasting locations (Greenland, Sweden and Kenya) using deep learning in microfluidic soil chips. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The study allows for the first time an automated visual investigation of soil bacterial communities. (certainty=high, type=contribution)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A crude biodiversity measure based on phenotypic cell morphology could become a valuable complement to molecular studies. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A crude biodiversity measure based on phenotypic cell morphology could become a valuable complement to molecular studies. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SnapCCESS is effective and more efficient than conventional ensemble deep learning-based clustering methods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SnapCCESS is effective and more efficient than conventional ensemble deep learning-based clustering methods. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SnapCCESS outperforms other state-of-the-art multimodal embedding generation methods in integrating data modalities for clustering cells. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SnapCCESS outperforms other state-of-the-art multimodal embedding generation methods in integrating data modalities for clustering cells. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The improved clustering of cells from SnapCCESS will pave the way for more accurate characterisation of cell identity and types. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The improved clustering of cells from SnapCCESS will pave the way for more accurate characterization of cell identity and types. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AutoComplete, a deep learning-based imputation method, can accurately impute missing phenotypes in population-scale Biobank datasets. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: AutoComplete, a deep learning-based imputation method, substantially improved imputation accuracy over existing methods when applied to phenotypes measured across ~300,000 individuals from the UK Biobank. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AutoComplete improved imputation accuracy over existing methods with an average improvement in r² of 18% for all phenotypes and 42% for binary phenotypes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: AutoComplete, a deep learning-based imputation method, substantially improved imputation accuracy over existing methods when applied to phenotypes measured across ~300,000 individuals from the UK Biobank. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the core claim of improved accuracy but frames it specifically for the UK Biobank application and omits the numeric r² breakdown; this is a clarification/narrowing rather than contradiction.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The application of AutoComplete to five clinically relevant traits with an average missingness rate of 83% resulted in an increase in effective sample size of approximately 2-fold on average. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: AutoComplete yields imputed phenotypes that are genetically similar to the originally observed phenotypes while increasing the effective sample size by about twofold on average. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the two-fold effective sample size increase and adds a genetic-similarity observation, but the original numeric result and scope are preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GWAS on the resulting imputed phenotypes led to an increase in the total number of loci significantly associated with the traits from four to 129. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Genome-wide association analyses on the resulting imputed phenotypes led to a substantial increase in the number of associated loci. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same result (substantial increase in associated loci) but omits the specific numeric change (4 to 129), so the claim is presented more generally.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Sequence UNET can classify and predict variant frequency directly from protein sequence. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Sequence UNET is a highly scalable deep learning architecture that classifies and predicts variant frequency from sequence alone. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the core capability (predicting variant frequency from sequence) and emphasizes scalability as an added characterization, making the original claim clearer.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Sequence UNET achieves comparable performance on pathogenicity prediction to methods including EVE and ESM-1b at greatly reduced computational cost. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Sequence UNET achieves comparable pathogenicity prediction to recent methods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication maintains the comparative pathogenicity claim but slightly generalizes the set of comparators; the substantive performance claim is preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Sequence UNET demonstrates scalability by analyzing the consequences of 8.3 billion variants in 904,134 proteins. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Sequence UNET demonstrates scalability by analyzing 8.3B variants in 904,134 proteins detected through large-scale proteomics. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numerical scalability result is retained; the publication adds that the proteins were detected through large-scale proteomics, which clarifies and provides additional context.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is a link between conservation and protein abundance as shown by the analysis conducted with Sequence UNET. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not include or repeat the claimed link between conservation and protein abundance, so this conclusion from the preprint appears to have been dropped.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed platform demonstrates high accuracy (>0.99 in each of two studies) in profiling substrate preferences for PTM enzymes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The integrated platform for profiling substrate preferences for PTM enzymes demonstrates high accuracy (>0.99 in each of two studies). (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep learning models resulting from the platform can quantify fitness across sequence space and identify important amino acids in the substrate. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep learning models can quantify fitness across sequence space, map modification sites, and identify important amino acids in the substrate. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the original capabilities and additionally mentions mapping modification sites, broadening the described functionality while keeping confidence level.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Substrate specificity profiling of the Ser dehydratase (LazBF) and Cys/Ser cyclodehydratase (LazDEF) reveals highly complex enzymatic preferences. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Profiling of the Ser dehydratase (LazBF) and Cys/Ser cyclodehydratase (LazDEF) reveals complex enzymatic preferences that cannot be reduced to simple rules. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ability of the constructed models to analyze complex enzymatic preferences suggests that the platform can facilitate the wider study of PTM enzymes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed platform can facilitate a wider study of PTM enzymes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The concluding claim is carried forward in the publication with equivalent meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepSCAb is a deep learning method that predicts inter-residue geometries as well as side chain dihedrals of the antibody variable fragment.
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepSCAb improves antibody structure prediction with accurate side-chain modeling.
